coredata.dc:title: Application of bioactive cyclic oligosaccharide on the detection of doxorubicin hydrochloride in unprocessed human plasma sample: A new platform towards efficient chemotherapy
coredata.dc:creator: Alizadeh, Parastoo Mohammad
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Soleymani, Jafar
Gharamaleki, Jalil Vaez
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel nano-composite based on poly‑taurine/beta‑cyclodextrin and graphene quantum dot used for the modification of gold electrode by simple electrodeposition approach. The prepared interface was applied for the sensing of doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) in human plasma samples without any pretreatment procedure. The synergistic influence of poly-taurine, beta-cyclodextrin, and graphene quantum dot not only remarkably enhances electroactivity, but also speeds up the electron shift between DOX and the electrode substrates. Cyclic voltammetry results indicated that the DOX is oxidized via two electrons and two protons process at the optimum pH = 4 using poly-taurine/beta-cyclodextrin and graphene quantum dots thin film modified gold electrode. More momentously electrostatic repulsion takes place between the prepared polymeric film and DOX resulting in the signal amplification on oxidation of this drug and lowering its overpotential and thereby sensitive and selective detection of DOX in real samples. The apparent electron transfer rate constant and transfer coefficients were determined by cyclic voltammetry. Using differential-pulse voltammetric (DPV) technique sensitive determination of DOX in human plasma samples was performed with wide dynamic range and good limit of detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioactive cyclic oligosaccharide
Doxorubicin hydrochloride
Cancer
Chemotherapy
Drug metabolism
Bioanalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the βCD/PT/GQD on bare Au electrode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran . This study was supported by Tabriz University of Medical Science as PHARMD thesis of Parastoo Mohammad Alizadeh.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CVs of a PT/βCD-GQD modified Au electrode in PBS (A) and (PBS+ 8.6 μMDOX) (B). Scan rate is 100 mV/s.
Inter-day precision, accuracy, and recoveries for determination of DOX in the standard and human plasma samples.
Details of the stability of the proposed method in whole blood and plasma samples.

head.ce:title: Application of bioactive cyclic oligosaccharide on the detection of doxorubicin hydrochloride in unprocessed human plasma sample: A new platform towards efficient chemotherapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Student Research Committee, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hematology-Oncology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel nano-composite based on poly‑taurine/beta‑cyclodextrin and graphene quantum dot used for the modification of gold electrode by simple electrodeposition approach. The prepared interface was applied for the sensing of doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) in human plasma samples without any pretreatment procedure. The synergistic influence of poly-taurine, beta-cyclodextrin, and graphene quantum dot not only remarkably enhances electroactivity, but also speeds up the electron shift between DOX and the electrode substrates. Cyclic voltammetry results indicated that the DOX is oxidized via two electrons and two protons process at the optimum pH = 4 using poly-taurine/beta-cyclodextrin and graphene quantum dots thin film modified gold electrode. More momentously electrostatic repulsion takes place between the prepared polymeric film and DOX resulting in the signal amplification on oxidation of this drug and lowering its overpotential and thereby sensitive and selective detection of DOX in real samples. The apparent electron transfer rate constant and transfer coefficients were determined by cyclic voltammetry. Using differential-pulse voltammetric (DPV) technique sensitive determination of DOX in human plasma samples was performed with wide dynamic range and good limit of detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Out-of-control cell growth and division of normal cells coupled with the ability to circulate in different parts of the body lead to a traumatic class of diseases named cancer. The unhindered grow, divide and re-divide instead of dying of cell's results in damage to deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and subsequently gene mutations [1]. Anthracycline antibiotics are an important group of chemotherapeutic agents, which displays a broad spectrum of antitumor activity, and doxorubicin (DOX) is one of the commonly used drugs against breast cancer, lung cancer, Hodgkin's and non-Hodgkin's lymphomas and sarcomas [2].

Similar to other neoplastic agents, nausea, vomiting, alopecia, stomatitis, and myelosuppression is DOX's acute toxicities but the long-term use of DOX is limited by cumulative, dose-related, progressive myocardial damage characterized by myocyte damage and myofibrillar loss that may lead to congestive heart failure (CHF) [3–9]. A large-scale study that retrospectively evaluated the cardiotoxicity of DOX revealed that an estimated 7% of patients developed DOX related congestive heart failure (CHF) after a cumulative dose of 450–550 mg/m2 with an ultimate mortality rate of 20–40%. However, now, we know DOX-related CHF occurs in greater frequency and at a lower accumulative dose than previously reported [10–12]. Therefore, it is critically consequential to develop novel electrode substances for sensitive determination of the trace amounts of DOX. Hence various analytical techniques have been used, and briefly can be listed as high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with varied detectors such as UV–Vis [13], tandem MS [14,15], chemiluminescence [16], fluorometric [17] and electroanalytical [17] detectors. The capillary electrophoresis coupled with a laser-induced fluorescent detector [18–20] is noted as another powerful hyphenated measurement technique for the separation and trace level determination of DOX, and its intermediates in complex biological matrices, as well UV–Vis spectrophotometry [21], fluorometry [22,23], Raman's spectroscopy [24], immunoassay analysis [25], and electroanalytical measurement techniques [26–48]. By the comparison to other methods, electrochemical devices, offering low expense, portability, and multiplexed capability. The sensitivity and selectivity of nano-sized materials based electrochemical sensors are advantages for these procedures and let them become special tools for analyzing a small amount of complicated mixtures without the instant necessity of pretreatment process [49–56].

Taurine (2-aminoethanesulfonic acid) is a famous sulfur-containing dissociated amino acid, which displays significant physiological roles and pharmacological attributes [57,58]. The unique structure and physicochemical properties of cyclic oligomers called β‑cyclodextrins make them one of the agents who used in pharmaceutical industry, food technology, and agriculture [59]. The Property of the host-guest interaction due to inclusion complexes with many appropriately sized organic and inorganic ions and molecules in aqueous, non-aqueous and mixed media by noncovalent interactions and the property of the increase in geometric surface area, making them an alternative choice in the molecular identification and selective preconcentration of the analyte at the electrode surface [52,59,60].

In summary, these improved performances encouraged us to explore the possible leading role played by the presence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the structure of poly(taurine‑β‑CD). Since the increase in geometric surface area is very important parameter in electrochemistry, therefore modification of poly(taurine‑β‑CD) with GQDs can increase the rate of electrochemical reaction. Therefore, integration of GQDs into poly(taurine‑β‑CD) can be providing an electrically conductive film towards increases Faradic currents. It was for these reasons that we decided to explore the possibility of using the poly(taurine‑β‑CD)‑GQDs) as a platform for the development of electrochemical sensor towards quantification of some DOX. Finally, we investigated the fully electrochemical preparation of poly(taurine‑β‑CD)‑GQDs) on the surface of Au electrode as a novel strategy for interface science. The formation of poly(taurine-β-CD)-GQDs) was firstly monitored. The latent activity of poly(taurine‑β‑CD)‑GQDs) coating towards detection and determination of DOX was investigated. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of the determination of DOX based on their direct electrochemical oxidation on poly(taurine‑β‑CD)‑GQDs) films.

All chemical compounds were procured from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) and used without additional refinement. Doxorubicin hydrochloride was purchased from Exir Nano Sina Company (Tehran, Iran) and all solutions were set with deionized water.

The stock solution of DOX (0.18 M) was prepared by dissolving an accurate amount of bulk drug in an appropriate volume of 0.02 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS), pH = 4.0 (which was also used as supporting electrolyte), and then stored in the dark place at 4 °C.

Additional diluted solutions were prepared daily by accurate dilution just before use. Also, the other stock solutions were prepared by dissolving an accurate amount equal to the molecular weight of each one in an appropriate volume of 1000 mL deionized water and then all of them stored in the dark place at 4 °C.

Fresh frozen plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran) and aliquots were transferred into microtubes and frozen at −4 °C until analysis. They were defreezed at room temperature every day and vortexed to ensure uniformity after thawing the samples gently, 0.5 mL of an aliquot volume of this sample was spiked with DOX and added into supporting electrolytes to reach a total volume of 5 mL.

Electrochemical measurements were utilized in a conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) designed by an electrochemical system including of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using NOVA 1.11 software. Further, an AC voltage with 20 mV peak-to-peak amplitude besides a 5 s equilibrium time was applied. An Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm), and a platinum wire was used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a Gold electrode (d = 2 mm) (From Azar electrode Co., Iran). For DPV calculations an initial potential of −0.8 V, end potential of 0.3 V, modulation amplitude of 0.025 V, modulation time of 0.025S, interval time 0.1S, a scan rate of 50 MV/s was utilized. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes was computed with a MIRA3 FEG-SEM (SEM, Hitachi Ltd., Czech).

A simple bottom-up approach was employed as the preparation of GQDs. Pyrolyzing of citric acid and its dispersion into alkaline solution lead to GQDs preparation. 2.000 g of citric acid has placed in a beaker and heated to 200 °C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, for preparing GQDs, 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution was added to the orange homogenous liquid dropwise with continuous stirring. The obtained GQDs solution was stable for maximum one month at 4 °C.

Before electrode modification, the bare gold electrode was well polished with aluminum polishing cloth for about 5 min. Later the electrode was plunged in 1:1 water/acetone mixture and cleansed with deionized water to annihilate organic contaminations; bare gold electrode was immersed in 0.1 M H2SO4/0.1 M HNO3 and then rinsed with deionized water to remove non-organic contaminants. The electrode dries out on room temperature.

This ultra-thin layer was fabricated by electrochemical polymerization of Poly (β-CD/taurine) at bare Au, by CV method in 0.1 M PBS containing 0.1 M taurine 0.00035 β-CD at pH = 6.8. Electropolymerization was performed between the potential of −1.5 to 2 V vs. Ag/AgCl at the scan rate of 100 mV/s in 10 cycles (Fig. S1A (see Supporting information)). The peak current increased by increasing the number of CV scans indicating that an electroconductive polymer film was formed on the electrode surface. Finally, the oxidation peaks unchanged after complete formation of PT/β-CD film. After 10 cycles of CV sweeps, the fabrication procedure was continued by electrodeposition of GQDs on thin layer of Poly (TA/β-CD) coated on Au electrode from GQDs aqueous solution in the potential range from 0.0 to 1.0 V at the scan rate of 100 mV (Fig. S1B (see Supporting information)). After 30 repeating cycles, the electrode was washed with distilled water to remove the physically adsorbed materials.

The electrochemical oxidation of DOX at the bare Au and modified Au electrode was investigated by the CV technique. CVs obtained in the presence of 8. 6 mM DOX (PBS, pH = 4) at a potential sweep rate of 100 mV/s, at the bare Au (curve a) and PT/Β-CD/GQD coated Au (curve b) in the presence of DOX. CVs results show that using PT/Β-CD/GQD coated Au electrode one pair of redox peaks appear. The comparison of recorded CVs using PT/Β-CD/GQD coated Au and bare Au electrode indicates a new anodic peak at −0.550 V vs. Ag/AgCl on the surface of PT/Β-CD/GQD which obviously shows the important role of biopolymer film in the electro oxidation of DOX (Fig. S2 (see Supporting information)). On the other word, these results were ascribed to the satisfactory electro activity of PT/β-CD/GQD coated Au which was due to the excellent characteristics of the polymer such as good electrical conductivity, high surface area and more electroactive interaction sites that can facilitate the electron transfer and charge exchange between the DOX and the modified electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the βCD/PT/GQD on bare Au electrode

ce:section.ce:para: The inclusive outward appearance morphologies of βCD/PT/GQD on bare Au electrodes are characterized using FE-SEM procedure. From the FE-SEM images, it can be distinguished that the modified βCD/PT layer (Fig. S3(A–C) (see Supporting information) is consistently distributed over the smooth Au electrode face. In addition, the GQD particles (2–5 nm) are also homogeneously dispensed over the surface of βCD/PT layer to form the three-dimensional structure, the 3D structure of βCD/PT/GQD nanocomposite exhibits stronger electrochemical activity and cumulation abilities, i.e., the increase of active surface area and the peak current of DOX. Besides, the EDS analyses was conducted for the βCD/PT/GQD electrode to ascertain the chemical composition of obtained nanocomposites (Fig. 3D (see Supporting information)). The former presents peaks for Au, C, N, O, P and S elements, of which Au element is from Au electrode; O elements should be derived from the βCD and PT. And the latter presents additional peaks for N and S apart from the PT. The C element is common between all. In general, the above results suggest that βCD/PT/GQD is effectively electrodeposited on the Au electrode surface.

Morphological characterization of the general surface morphologies of bare and modified Au electrodes, by poly taurin/β-CD, and GQDs. electrodes are differentiated using SEM technique. From the SEM images, it can be perceived that the modified PT/Β-CD layer is consistently distributed over the smooth Au electrode face Moreover; the GQD particles (2–5 nm) are also homogeneously distributed over the surface of pt./Β-CD layer to generate the 3D configuration. This property of PT/Β-CD and GQD nanocomposite exhibits higher electrochemical reactivity and stronger accumulation abilities, i.e., the increase of active surface area and the peak current of DOX.

In order to verify the electroactivity of the modified electrode for determination of 8. 6 mM DOX (PBS, pH = 4) in plasma samples, the electrochemical oxidation of DOX at the bare and modified Au electrode were investigated by CV technique. Fig. 1 shows CVs of PT/Β-CD/GQD modified Au electrode in the absence and presence of DOX, while potential sweep rate of 100 mV/s was employed. The contrast of recorded CVs in the absence and presence of DOX exhibits a new anodic peak at −0.460 V on the surface of the modified electrode. This result displays the acceptable electroactivity of PT/β-CD/GQD due to the extraordinary characteristics of the biopolymer. This biopolymer increases the electroactive fundamental interaction sites which can help electron transporting and charges changing between DOX and adjusted electrode surface. This modifier has lowered the oxidation peak potential and also increased the peak current. Electrostatic repulsion/attraction of DOX on the electropolymerized film over the surface of the electrodes after the composition of electropolymerized film over the electrode is due to hydrogen bond forms between hydrogen in hydroxyphenol on the benzene ring of DOX and the nitrogen in the system of polypurine and OH groups in β-CD.

DOX displayed very feeble redox pulse within the potential window from −0.34 to −0.52 V at the bare Au surface. The peak current of DOX increased by administration of PT/β-CD/GQD. This signal enhancement may be ascribed to the excellent conduction and large surface area of GQD. On the other hand, β-CD on the surface of Au electrode can form an inclusion complex with DOX. This inclusion can further result in a remarkable peak current. The hydrogen bond activates hydroxy phenol on the benzene ring of DOX, leading to enfeebling of the bored energy between hydrogen and oxygen. The bond of the benzene ring and oxygen of DOX is prone to break and as the oxidation process accelerates it leads to the enhancement of peak current of DOX electrooxidation at PT-Au surface. The other active functional groups at the surface of the electrode can easily form hydrogen bonds with the amine groups of DOX; which weaken the NR bond energies enhancement leads to good electrocatalytic activating for redox reaction of DOX and it is a good mediator to shuttle electrons between DOX and the working electrode.

The pH of supporting electrolyte affects the peak currents and peak potentials of DOX. The influence of the phosphate buffer solution pH on the electro-oxidation of DOX (8.6 mM of DOX) was examined over the pH range of 3–10 (Fig. S4A (see Supporting information)). It was observed that the anodic peak current (Ipa) reached its maximum rate at pH = 4. Therefore, pH = 4 was chosen as the optimum pH condition for the following electrochemical measurements. In this study, it was found that the oxidation peak potential of DOX shifted to fewer negative potentials with an increase in the pH of a medium (Fig. S4B (see Supporting information)). The pH dependence of Epa can be expressed by the linear regression equation as Epa(V) = −0.0541pH-0.1855 (R2 = 0.997) (Fig. S4C (see Supporting information)), indicating that the proton is involved in the electrochemical reaction of DOX on PT/β-CD and GQD.

The calculated slope of −0.0541 V pH−1 is close to the theoretical value of 0.059 which indicates that the number of two electrons and two protons should be in the electrochemical oxidation of DOX at the proposed interface and is equal according to Nernst equation.

In order to explain the electrochemical mechanism of DOX on the surface of PT/β-CD/GQD electrode, the influence of scan rate (v) in the absence and presence response of DOX is conducted by CV technique, at scan rates over the range of 2–1000 mV/s. It can be observed that there is no oxidation peak for PT/β-CD/GQD in the absence of DOX in different scan rates. On the other hand, there is a pair of irreversible redox peaks and the oxidation peak currents of PT/Β-CD/GQD in the presence of DOX are enhanced as the scan rate increased (Fig. S5 (see Supporting information)).

As illustrated in (Fig. S6 (see Supporting information)) the linear regression equation can be expressed as Ipa (μA) = 22.441 v 1/2 (Vs−1) − 1.65 (R2 = 0.992). This behavior suggests that the electrooxidation process of DOX controlled diffusion which was confirmed by a linear relation between Ipa and square roots of scan rate (Fig. S6 (see Supporting information)). In addition, there was a linear relation between lnIpa and ln ν, corresponding to the following equation: lnIpa (μA) = 0.5628 lnν (Vs−1) + 3.0312 (R2 = 0.9973). The slope of 0.56 indicates that the electrode process is almost absolutely controlled by diffusion (Fig. S6 (see Supporting information)). A slope close to 0.5 is expected for diffusion-controlled electrode processes and close to 1.0 for adsorption-controlled processes [42].

Fig. S6 (see Supporting information) presents a dependence of Epa on scan rate determined from cyclic voltammogram recorded for the DOX electrooxidation at PT/βCD-GQD modified Au electrode. If electrochemical reaction is irreversible, then Epa is independent on v. Thus, it can be concluded that heterogeneous electron transfer in selected drugs electrooxidation is irreversible because Epa increases with an increase in the scan rate. In addition, the value of the overall electron transfer coefficient for the reaction can be obtained from the following equation [43–45]:
(1)
Ep =
RT
2 αF
ln υ + constant
where Ep—peak potential (V), R—universal gas constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1), F—Faraday constant (96,487 C mol−1), T—Kelvin temperature (298 K), α—anodic transfer coefficient, v—scan rate (V·s−1).

Using the relation of anodic peak potential on the logarithm of the potential scan rate (Fig. S6 (see Supporting information)), the value of the overall electron transfer coefficient (α n) was obtained as 0.61 for electrooxidation of DOX.

As shown in Fig. S7, the first step of modification had a saturation value for the amount of PT/Β-CD/GQD on the surface of the Au electrode. The peak current increased by adding 0.00035 M of β-cyclodextrin. A higher concentration of B-CD showed a decrease in peak current. This is presumably due to the reduction of the conductivity of the sensor due to the saturation of Β-CD/Au surface. Polytaurine (0.1 M) in PBS (0.1 M) was chosen as the optimized amount according to previous articles [76].

Under the optimal conditions, the determination of DOX was carried out using DPV method because of its higher current sensitivity and better resolution compared to CV. Fig. S8A (see Supporting information) shows the DPV responses of PT/β-CD/GQD on Au electrode towards various concentrations of DOX. A well-defined DPV response is noticed upon addition of DOX, and the oxidation peak currents exhibit a linear response with respect to the DOX concentration as shown in Fig. S8B (see Supporting information). The influence of experimental parameters was continued to find the optimum waiting time of DOX electrooxidation at PT/Β-CD/GQD modified Au electrode. It was observed that the relative peak current increases until approaching to a constant value after about 20 min. After 20 min, due to the saturation of the electrode surface, electrode response was constant. Thus, in all following Experiments incubation time of 10 min was selected as the optimum condition.

The corresponding regression equation was I(μA) = 0.2217 CDOX(μM) + 0.0107 (R2 = 0.9983) for electrooxidation of DOX at PT/Β-CD/GQD modified Au electrode. The linear range from 0.086 μM to 3.45 μM was obtained which the LOD was 0.012 μM, LOQ was 0.04 μM and LLOQ was 0.086 μM. Compared to the earlier reported DOX sensors in terms of linear range and LOD, the present sensor shows good performance, that is, the appropriate linear range and lower LOD, as shown in Table S1 (see Supporting information). The reported range of detection and LOD are suitable for sensing DOX concentration in the human biofluids (3–900 nM).

Although in some cases, the previous methods [61-75] as shown in Table S1 (see Supporting information), provide the better limits of detection, it is important to mention that the reported methods were mostly utilized the assay in human plasma sample with the complicated and time-consuming sample preparation steps.

FDA validation standards strongly suggest the inter-day and intra-day evaluation to display the accuracy of the reported method where three concentrations (low, mid and high) of DOX were tried f intra-day and inter-day exactness. Five replicated analyses of plasma aliquots of DOX concentrations of 0.14–0.86 and 3.45 μM (for plasma) were executed. Table 1 indicated the inter-day and intra-day exactness accuracy and recovery of the process in human plasma samples. Respecting the maximum FDA allowed coefficient of variation value of 15%; the process has satisfactory accuracy and precision.
Three electrodes for each of the low and high concentrations (0.14 μM and 0.86 μM) kept at room temperature for 4 h and then analyzed.

The stability of the proposed sensor determined after three freeze and thaw cycles. Three electrodes for each of the low and high concentrations (0.14 μM and 0.86 μM) stored at −4C° for 24 h and thawed unassisted at room temperature. When completely thawed, the electrode refroze for 12 to 24 h under the same conditions. The freeze-thaw cycle repeated two more times, then analyzed on the third cycle (Table 2 ).

The selectivity of the prepared sensor around interfering substitutes was examined where the effects of an extensive diversity of coexisting substances over the DPV signal of the PT/β-CD/GQD on bare Au electrode were checked in human plasma samples. The electrode was tested on the promising obtrusive elements; which can effects on DPV signal of the coated electrode. The probably officious agents and their edge concentrations and also the current to the probe in existence and non-existence of the interfering agents. The interfering effect was computed by the differences among the peak current in attendance and absenteeism of the meddling agents.

This study presents a rapid and sensitive platform for detecting trace amounts of DOX in human plasma samples by combining advantages of PT, β-CD, and GQD in one interface. The proposed sensor has been displayed to be a hopeful method for DOX detection, and the limit of detection was improved with the accumulation of the analyte on the surface and further, it will be better by utilizing 10 min waiting time. As a cost-effective and convenient operation sensor with many qualified properties, including high durability, good reproducibility, low detection limit and fast response time, the proposed interface was used for quantification of DOX in human plasma samples, without requiring any sample pretreatment or time-consuming extraction or evaporation steps prior to the analysis. Therefore, it can be considered as a great utility for some real applications, such as pharmacokinetic investigations in cancer patients.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran. This study was supported by Tabriz University of Medical Science as PHARMD thesis of Parastoo Mohammad Alizadeh.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2018.11.012.
